blocks.content: That had been the plan—walk back, maybe stop by The Copper Mug if it was still open, sit for a while, let the day settle into something he could understand.
But instead, he found himself walking slower than usual, the city stretching around him in quiet, familiar ways that no longer felt entirely the same.
Gerald hung loosely from his hand, the duck's crooked bow tie catching the last of the evening light like it had something to prove.
He exhaled softly and looked ahead.
Everything looked exactly like it had that morning.
Same buildings. Same sidewalks. Same muted colors of a town that never tried too hard to impress anyone.
Ethan slowed near the park without meaning to.
The fountain sat in its usual place, water still sputtering upward in that uneven, stubborn way it always had.
But now it wasn't just a fountain.
He stepped closer, stopping just at the edge of the stone path.
For a moment, he could almost see it again—
The way she had looked at his painting like it mattered.
The way she spoke like moments weren't small things to pass through, but something to actually step into.
Ethan rubbed the back of his neck.
It was just a couple of days,
That shouldn't be enough to change anything.
He looked down at Gerald again.
Then finally let out a quiet breath.
Ethan turned away from the fountain and started walking again.
But not like he was leaving something behind.
More like he was carrying it with him.
